content:
Ryan, next Sunday I’d do two things at once: shepherd my child and honor the Scriptures.

In the pew, while Psalm 19 is read:
- I’d quietly point to the text and whisper, “This is a poem, and it’s all true.” Then I’d trace two anchors from the psalm:
  - Verses 1–6: Creation really does “declare” God’s glory. The sun doesn’t live in an actual tent; that’s poetic language. But the truth it communicates is objective: God’s world is shouting His greatness.
  - Verses 7–11: God’s written word is perfect, trustworthy, and righteous altogether. We don’t trim it for PR; we receive it.
- After the reading I’d say, “Poetry can use pictures to tell true things. The same God who made the heavens also spoke in Scripture, and we listen carefully to what He actually wrote.”

At lunch with my 10-year-old, Bible open:
- I’d read Psalm 19 again and then read Genesis 1:1–5. I’d show the difference between poetry (parallel lines, rich imagery) and narrative prose (the repeated, time-anchored sequence: “And God said… and it was so… and there was evening and there was morning, the nth day”). Genesis 1 is elevated, patterned prose, but it reads as history, not Hebrew poetry.
- I’d connect Scripture with Scripture:
  - Exodus 20:11 ties the creation week to our week: “For in six days the LORD made heaven and earth…” That’s given as the reason for Sabbath rhythm.
  - Jesus treats Genesis as history: “From the beginning of creation, ‘God made them male and female’” (Mark 10:6).
  - Paul treats Adam as a real man in Romans 5 and 1 Corinthians 15.
- I’d say, “We love brothers and sisters who see details differently, but we never call something ‘poetry’ just to make people like us. We ask, ‘What did God actually say, and how did He say it?’”

About the elder’s comment:
- I would not correct him from the pew or sour my child’s spirit. I’d set up a gracious, private conversation that week. I’d ask:
  - “When you say ‘for unity,’ do you mean the text itself demands a poetic reading, or are we choosing it to avoid offense?”
  - “How do you see the genre markers of Psalm 19 versus Genesis 1?”
  - “How do you read Exodus 20:11, Mark 10:6, Romans 5?” 
  - “When Scripture speaks of a ‘stumbling block,’ it’s Christ crucified (1 Corinthians 1:23). Are we in danger of removing the wrong offense?”
- I’d make it clear I want unity in truth: we can show charity on age-of-earth discussions, but we must not re-label a passage to mute its claims. Inerrancy means all Scripture is true in the way each passage intends to speak.

What I want my 10-year-old to carry home:
- All Scripture is God’s Word and therefore trustworthy.
- Different genres speak truly in different ways; metaphors are real communication, not make-believe.
- We don’t adjust the Bible to fit the room; we adjust ourselves to fit the Bible.
- We honor our leaders, pray for them, and test everything by the Word like the Bereans (Acts 17:11).

Then we’d memorize a verse together—either Psalm 19:1 or Psalm 19:7—and thank God that His world and His Word never contradict, because He is the Author of both.
